title: What Is OpenRouter? One Hosted API for Every Model
seoTitle: What Is OpenRouter? One Hosted API for All LLMs
metaDescription: OpenRouter is a hosted unified API to hundreds of models from many providers, with automatic failover. Learn what it does and how it works.
oneLiner: After reading, you'll understand what OpenRouter is, how its single hosted API reaches hundreds of models, and how its automatic failover keeps apps running.

blocks.md: Every model provider speaks its own dialect. OpenAI wants one request shape and one API key; Anthropic wants another; Google, Mistral, Meta's hosts, and a dozen smaller labs each want their own. If your app calls three of them, you wire up three SDKs, juggle three keys, pay three separate bills, and rewrite code every time you want to try a new model.

**OpenRouter** removes that friction. It is a *hosted* service that sits between your app and hundreds of models from many providers, exposing all of them through **one** API endpoint, **one** API key, and **one** bill. You change the model you call by changing a single string like `anthropic/claude-sonnet-4` to `openai/gpt-5` — no new SDK, no new account, no code rewrite.

Think of it like a travel-booking site. You don't open separate accounts with thirty airlines, learn each of their websites, and pay each one directly. You search one site, it shows you every airline's flights side by side, you book through it, and it handles the airline behind the scenes. OpenRouter is that single front desk for language models: one place to reach almost any model, with the provider-specific plumbing hidden away.

A *gateway* (also called an LLM router or proxy) is any layer that takes a model request and forwards it to the right provider. OpenRouter is the **hosted, managed** kind — someone else runs the servers. The opposite is a *self-hosted* gateway like LiteLLM, which does similar routing but runs on infrastructure you own and operate.

The model landscape changes constantly. A model that is the best choice for your task this month may be beaten on price or quality next month. Builders who hard-wire their app to one provider pay a real cost for that lock-in, and OpenRouter exists to remove it.

items: **One integration, every model.** You write the integration once against an OpenAI-compatible endpoint. After that, trying a new model is a one-line change, not a new project. This makes it cheap to experiment and to switch when something better ships.

**One key, one bill.** Instead of signing contracts and managing billing with each lab, you top up a single balance and OpenRouter settles with the providers. For a small team or a side project, skipping that paperwork is the whole value.

**Resilience through failover.** If a provider is down, rate-limiting you, or slow, OpenRouter can automatically retry the same request on another provider that serves the same model. Your app keeps answering instead of returning errors.
**No infrastructure to run.** There is nothing to deploy, scale, or patch. You don't operate a proxy server — that responsibility is the service's, which is the core trade-off versus self-hosting.

blocks.md: Who should care? Anyone building on top of LLMs who wants optionality without operational overhead: prototypes and hackathon projects, indie apps, internal tools, and products that want to A/B-test models or route different tasks to different models. If you need to stay model-agnostic but don't want to run your own routing layer, a hosted gateway is the path of least resistance.

What it replaces is the pile of provider-specific SDKs and accounts you would otherwise glue together by hand — and the brittle, custom failover logic teams write to survive a provider outage. OpenRouter turns both into a configuration detail.

Mechanically, OpenRouter is a smart proxy. Your app sends a normal chat-completion request to OpenRouter's endpoint, naming the model you want. OpenRouter looks at which providers can serve that model, picks one according to your routing preferences, translates your request into that provider's native format, forwards it, translates the response back, and streams it to you. If the chosen provider fails, it tries the next eligible one.

diagram.title: One request, from your app to a model and back

blocks.md: OpenRouter mimics the OpenAI chat-completions API. That choice matters: countless tools and SDKs already speak that format, so you usually "connect" by pointing your existing OpenAI client at OpenRouter's base URL and swapping in an OpenRouter key. The `model` field carries a `provider/model` slug to say which model you want.

blocks.title: calling OpenRouter with the OpenAI SDK
blocks.code:
from openai import OpenAI

# Same SDK you'd use for OpenAI — only the base URL and key change.
client = OpenAI(
    base_url="https://openrouter.ai/api/v1",
    api_key="sk-or-...",   # one OpenRouter key, not per-provider keys
)

resp = client.chat.completions.create(
    model="anthropic/claude-sonnet-4",   # provider/model slug
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "Explain failover in one line."}],
)
print(resp.choices[0].message.content)

# Switch models by editing ONE string — no new SDK, no new key:
#   model="openai/gpt-5"
#   model="google/gemini-2.5-pro"

blocks.md: Popular models are often hosted by several providers at once. OpenRouter treats those as interchangeable backends for the same model and chooses between them using your preferences — for example, cheapest first, lowest-latency first, or a fixed order you specify. If the first attempt errors out or times out, it falls back to the next provider automatically. That is the *failover* that keeps an app alive during a single provider's bad day.

diagram.title: Failover: one model, several interchangeable backends

blocks.md: Because the response format is normalized, your code doesn't change when failover happens behind the scenes. The app just sees a successful answer — it never learns which provider actually served it unless you check the response metadata.

OpenRouter is one end of a spectrum. The other end is running your own gateway — most commonly LiteLLM, an open-source proxy that also gives you one unified API across providers, but on servers you control. Both solve "talk to many models through one interface"; they differ in who carries the weight.

rows: Sign up, get one key, change base URL
Deploy and maintain the proxy yourself
One OpenRouter key; it holds the rest
You supply and store each provider's key
Requests pass through a third party
Requests stay inside your own network

blocks.md: Neither is strictly better. Pick the hosted route when you want to move fast, keep optionality, and avoid operating anything. Pick self-hosting when you must keep traffic and credentials inside your own boundary, want to negotiate provider rates directly, or need routing rules a managed service won't give you. Some teams even use a hosted gateway to prototype and migrate to a self-hosted one as scale and compliance needs grow.

Beyond routing, OpenRouter acts as a *marketplace*: a single catalog where you can browse models from many providers and compare them before you commit a single line of code. That discovery layer is a real part of the value, because it turns "which model and which host?" into a browsable list rather than a research project.

items: **Breadth of catalog.** Frontier closed models, strong open-weight models, and niche or specialized ones live side by side under consistent `provider/model` slugs.
**Compare before you call.** You can weigh models on context window, capabilities, and the providers that serve each one, then route by your own priority — cost, speed, or a preferred host.
**Graceful access to many providers.** Because one model may be offered by several hosts, you get redundancy for free: more places to fall back to when one is unavailable.

blocks.md: A unified slug like `meta-llama/llama-3.3-70b-instruct` is doing quiet work: it names a model independently of *who* runs it, which is exactly what lets OpenRouter swap one host for another without changing your request.

sections.title: When to use it — and what to watch for

blocks.md: A hosted gateway is a great default for staying model-agnostic with minimal effort, but the convenience comes with trade-offs worth understanding before you build on it.

items: **Reach for it when** you're prototyping, want one-line model switching, need many models without many accounts, or want automatic failover without writing it yourself.

**An extra network hop.** Your request travels through one more service before reaching the model, which can add a little latency. For most apps this is negligible; for ultra-latency-sensitive paths, measure it.

**A third party in the data path.** Your prompts and completions pass through the gateway. If you handle regulated or highly sensitive data, check the provider's data-handling and retention policy — this is often the deciding factor against a hosted option.
**A new dependency and bill.** You're now relying on one more service's uptime and pricing. Plan how your app behaves if the gateway itself is unavailable, and keep an eye on cost.

**Subtle provider differences.** Two hosts serving "the same" model can differ slightly in quantization, speed, or feature support. If consistency is critical, pin a specific provider rather than letting routing pick freely.

blocks.md: Failover is a reliability feature, not a free pass. Falling back to a different provider may change latency, cost, or behavior for that request. For workloads where the exact model and host must stay fixed, disable broad routing and pin your provider explicitly.

A gateway also pairs naturally with cost and latency techniques like [semantic caching](/learn/production-llmops/cost-latency-optimization/semantic-caching-llm/) and broader [token-cost reduction](/learn/production-llmops/cost-latency-optimization/cut-llm-token-costs/) — the gateway gets your request to a model, and those techniques keep that traffic cheap and fast.
Once the basics click, the interesting questions are about *how* to route, not just *whether* to. A few directions worth knowing.

**Routing strategy is a real lever.** "Cheapest available" maximizes savings but may bounce you between providers with different speeds; "lowest latency" or a pinned order trades some cost for predictability. Many teams route *by task*: a cheap, fast model for classification and short replies, a stronger model for hard reasoning. A gateway makes that policy a configuration choice instead of branching code.

**OpenAI-compatibility is the quiet superpower.** Because OpenRouter speaks the chat-completions format, most agent frameworks, eval harnesses, and observability tools work with it unchanged — you inherit a whole ecosystem by setting a base URL. The catch is that provider-specific features (certain tool-calling quirks, structured-output modes, or extended parameters) may not pass through identically across every model, so test the exact features you depend on per model.

**Hosted and self-hosted are not mutually exclusive.** A common pattern is to start on a hosted gateway for speed and breadth, then graduate to a self-hosted proxy such as LiteLLM (or place an edge proxy like [Cloudflare AI Gateway](/learn/production-llmops/cost-latency-optimization/cloudflare-ai-gateway/) in front) once you need tighter control over data, credentials, and routing. The unified-API mental model carries over cleanly from one to the other.

**Observability still matters.** A gateway centralizes your traffic, which is a perfect place to watch spend, latency, and error rates across models in one view. Tag your requests by feature or customer so the single bill stays explainable, and track which provider actually served each call so you can reason about behavior changes after a failover.

The durable idea is simple: a gateway decouples your *application* from any single *provider*. Whether hosted like OpenRouter or self-run like LiteLLM, that decoupling is what lets a fast-moving model landscape stay a one-line change instead of a migration project.

faq.a: It gives you a single hosted API, key, and bill to reach hundreds of models from many providers. You integrate once against an OpenAI-compatible endpoint, then switch models with a one-line change and get automatic failover between providers if one is down or slow.

faq.q: Is OpenRouter the same as LiteLLM?
faq.a: They solve the same problem — one unified API across many model providers — but differ in who runs it. OpenRouter is a hosted service on someone else's servers with one key and one bill. LiteLLM is open-source software you self-host, so you supply each provider's key and pay each provider directly. Choose hosted for speed and convenience, self-hosted for control and keeping data in your own network.

Many popular models are served by several providers at once. OpenRouter treats those as interchangeable backends and forwards your request to one based on your routing preference. If that attempt errors or times out, it automatically retries on another provider that serves the same model, so your app gets an answer instead of an error.

Yes. It mirrors the OpenAI chat-completions API, so you can usually keep your existing OpenAI SDK and just change the base URL to OpenRouter's and use an OpenRouter key. The model field takes a `provider/model` slug to pick which model you want.

It adds one extra network hop, since requests pass through the gateway before reaching the model. For most applications this overhead is small and outweighed by the convenience and failover. For latency-critical paths, measure it and consider pinning a fast provider.

faq.q: Is it safe to send sensitive data through OpenRouter?
faq.a: Your prompts and completions pass through a third-party service, so for regulated or highly sensitive data you should review its data-handling and retention policy first. Teams with strict compliance needs often prefer a self-hosted gateway that keeps traffic and credentials inside their own network.
